title: Exercise: Fix a Fragmented Assembly (Goose-foot Starfish)
introduction:
This is a self-directed fix-it exercise on a real, uncurated Darwin Tree of Life assembly of the goose-foot starfish (Anseropoda placenta), an echinoderm. There is no answer key: no expert has curated this genome, so you decide what to fix.

Unlike the clean cockle exercise, this assembly has a real problem to solve. It comes in two very different halves: a core of genuine chromosomes at the top-left, and a long tail of roughly 1,700 tiny contigs at the bottom-right that carry little Hi-C signal. Most of that tail is duplicate haplotype sequence (haplotigs) and unplaceable fragments, not missing chromosomes.

Your task:
1. Separate the real chromosomes from the unplaced tail.
2. Triage the tail: find the rare placeable contig, and recognise the haplotigs and junk.
3. Refine the chromosome core by hand.
4. Self-assess the result.

A note on the automated tools: on an overview-resolution map like this, auto-cut finds few or no breakpoints, and most flagged misassemblies are false positives. The real work here is manual judgment, not button-pressing.

steps.title: See the two halves
steps.instruction:
Scan the whole map and notice it has two zones:

• Top-left (about the first 55%): bright, square chromosome blocks. These are real chromosomes.
• Bottom-right (the rest): a pale, grey, striped tail of hundreds of tiny contigs with almost no block structure.

The insulation track above the map tells the same story: it has signal over the core and goes flat over the tail.
steps.hint: The minimap shows it at a glance: a solid diagonal in the top-left, fading to a faint smear in the bottom-right. That smear is the unplaced tail, and it is the main thing you are here to deal with.

steps.title: Zoom into the tail
steps.instruction:
Zoom into the bottom-right tail and look closely. You will see many short contigs and heavy flagging: purple haplotig markers and red weak-join markers. Weak joins mean the Hi-C evidence linking these pieces to anything is thin.

This is what an unplaced tail looks like: real but fragmentary sequence the scaffolder could not confidently place.
steps.hint: Do not try to place every tiny contig. Most of the tail is haplotigs (duplicate haplotype) and small fragments that will never join a chromosome cleanly.

steps.title: Triage the tail
steps.instruction:
Now sort the tail into three kinds:

• Placeable: a contig with a bright off-diagonal line pointing at a specific chromosome belongs there. These are rare but worth rescuing.
• Haplotig: duplicate haplotype sequence, already flagged in purple. These should be set aside, not placed.
• Unplaceable junk: tiny fragments with only weak, diffuse links.

Hunt for a placeable contig first; those are the highest-value finds.
steps.hint: A placeable contig lights up a clean vertical or horizontal streak into one chromosome block. A haplotig tends to echo an existing chromosome rather than extend it.

steps.title: Try auto-cut (and see its limits)
steps.instruction:
Run auto-cut (Alt+C or the command palette) and accept the default sensitivity. On this overview-resolution map it will likely find little or nothing to cut. That is expected: fine-scale misassemblies are not visible at this zoom, and most flags are false positives.

The lesson here is that automated tools are a starting point, not a substitute for looking.
steps.hint: If auto-cut proposes cuts you disagree with, undo them all from the command palette. Trust the map over the flags.

steps.title: Refine the chromosomes by hand
steps.instruction:
Turn to the core and fix real problems by hand:

• If a chromosome block shows a short anti-diagonal (a butterfly), that contig is inverted: cut it out and invert it.
• If two adjacent contigs have a strong shared signal across their boundary, join them.
• If you rescued a placeable contig from the tail, move it into its chromosome.

Work on the largest chromosomes first, where mistakes cost the most.
steps.hint: Press E for edit mode. Hover a contig and press C to cut, F to invert a selection, J to join. High-impact edits on big scaffolds matter far more than fiddling with the tail.

steps.title: Sort the core
steps.instruction: Run auto-sort (Alt+S) to order the chromosomes for the cleanest diagonal. Because the tail contigs carry little signal, focus your judgment on whether the large chromosome blocks are in a sensible order and orientation. Auto-sort handles order, not orientation, so check orientations yourself afterward.
steps.hint: After sorting, the real chromosomes should form a clean, bright staircase down the diagonal, with the unplaced tail collected at the end.

steps.title: Self-assess the result
steps.instruction:
There is no automated score for a species no one has curated yet. Judge it yourself:

• Are the real chromosomes clean, bright, square blocks in a sensible order?
• Did you rescue the contigs that clearly belonged in a chromosome?
• Are the haplotigs and junk set aside rather than forced into the assembly?
• Would you be comfortable calling the core, not the tail, the chromosome-level assembly?

Deciding what to leave in the tail is as much of the job as building the chromosomes.
steps.hint: A real curator ships the clean chromosomes and lists the rest as unplaced. A perfect diagonal over the core with a labelled tail is a success, not a failure.
